title: Why Your Brain Is Addicted to Bad News

lines.text: You open your phone to check the time — and 20 minutes later you're still scrolling bad news.
image_keywords: social media feed smartphone close up

lines.text: This isn't a willpower problem. Your brain is literally wired for it.
It's called negativity bias — your brain processes threats faster and stronger than good news.
For thousands of years, missing a threat meant death. Missing good news just meant a missed opportunity.
So your brain evolved to prioritize danger. Bad news feels more real, more urgent, more important.
Social media platforms learned this too — negative posts get more clicks, more shares, more time on screen.
image_keywords: social media apps icons phone screen

lines.text: The result: your ancient survival brain is being hijacked by an algorithm designed to keep you anxious.
Knowing this doesn't fix it — but it gives you the one thing the algorithm doesn't want you to have: awareness.
